    14ŌåÆlimitations under the License.
    15ŌåÆ-/
    16ŌåÆ
    17ŌåÆimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18ŌåÆ
    19ŌåÆ/-!
    20ŌåÆ# Erd┼æs Problem 782
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆ*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/782](https://www.erdosproblems.com/782)
    23ŌåÆ
    24ŌåÆA question of Brown, Erd┼æs, and Freedman [BEF90]. It is a classical fact that
    25ŌåÆthe squares do not contain arithmetic progressions of length 4. An affirmative
    26ŌåÆanswer to the first question implies an affirmative answer to the second.
    27ŌåÆ
    28ŌåÆ[BEF90] Brown, T. C., Erd┼æs, P., and Freedman, A. R., _Quasi-progressions and
    29ŌåÆdescending waves_. J. Combin. Theory Ser. A 53 (1990), no. 1, 81-95.
    30ŌåÆ-/
    31ŌåÆ
    32ŌåÆopen Finset
    33ŌåÆ
    34ŌåÆopen scoped BigOperators
    35ŌåÆ
    36ŌåÆnamespace Erdos782
    37ŌåÆ
    38ŌåÆ/--
    39ŌåÆErd┼æs Problem 782, Part 1 (Quasi-progressions in the squares) [BEF90]:
    40ŌåÆ
    41ŌåÆThere exists a constant $C$ such that for any $k \geq 2$, one can find $k$ perfect
    42ŌåÆsquares $x_1 < x_2 < \cdots < x_k$ forming a quasi-progression with gap $d$, meaning
    43ŌåÆ$x_i + d \leq x_{i+1} \leq x_i + d + C$ for all consecutive pairs.
    44ŌåÆ-/
    45ŌåÆ@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    46ŌåÆtheorem erdos_782 : answer(sorry) Ōåö
    47ŌåÆ    Ōłā C : ŌäĢ, ŌłĆ k : ŌäĢ, k Ōēź 2 ŌåÆ
    48ŌåÆ    Ōłā (x : Fin k ŌåÆ ŌäĢ) (d : ŌäĢ), d > 0 Ōł¦
    49ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ i, IsSquare (x i)) Ōł¦
    50ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ i j : Fin k, i < j ŌåÆ x i < x j) Ōł¦
    51ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ (i : ŌäĢ) (hi : i + 1 < k),
    52ŌåÆ      x Ō¤©i, by omegaŌ¤® + d Ōēż x Ō¤©i + 1, hiŌ¤® Ōł¦
    53ŌåÆ      x Ō¤©i + 1, hiŌ¤® Ōēż x Ō¤©i, by omegaŌ¤® + d + C) := by
    54ŌåÆ  sorry
    55ŌåÆ
    56ŌåÆ/--
    57ŌåÆErd┼æs Problem 782, Part 2 (Combinatorial cubes in the squares) [BEF90]:
    58ŌåÆ
    59ŌåÆFor any $m$, the set of perfect squares contains a combinatorial cube of
    60ŌåÆdimension $m$: there exist $a$ and positive $b_1, \ldots, b_m$ such that
    61ŌåÆ$a + \sum_{i \in S} b_i$ is a perfect square for every subset
    62ŌåÆ$S \subseteq \{1, \ldots, m\}$.
    63ŌåÆ-/
    64ŌåÆ@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    65ŌåÆtheorem erdos_782.variants.cubes : answer(sorry) Ōåö
    66ŌåÆ    ŌłĆ m : ŌäĢ, Ōłā (a : ŌäĢ) (b : Fin m ŌåÆ ŌäĢ),
    67ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ i, b i > 0) Ōł¦
    68ŌåÆ    (ŌłĆ S : Finset (Fin m), IsSquare (a + Ōłæ i Ōłł S, b i)) := by
    69ŌåÆ  sorry
    70ŌåÆ
    71ŌåÆend Erdos782
    72ŌåÆ

/-

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erd┼æs Problem 782

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/782](https://www.erdosproblems.com/782)

A question of Brown, Erd┼æs, and Freedman [BEF90]. It is a classical fact that
the squares do not contain arithmetic progressions of length 4. An affirmative
answer to the first question implies an affirmative answer to the second.

[BEF90] Brown, T. C., Erd┼æs, P., and Freedman, A. R., _Quasi-progressions and
descending waves_. J. Combin. Theory Ser. A 53 (1990), no. 1, 81-95.
-/

open Finset

open scoped BigOperators

namespace Erdos782

/--
Erd┼æs Problem 782, Part 1 (Quasi-progressions in the squares) [BEF90]:

There exists a constant $C$ such that for any $k \geq 2$, one can find $k$ perfect
squares $x_1 < x_2 < \cdots < x_k$ forming a quasi-progression with gap $d$, meaning
$x_i + d \leq x_{i+1} \leq x_i + d + C$ for all consecutive pairs.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
theorem erdos_782 : answer(sorry) Ōåö
    Ōłā C : ŌäĢ, ŌłĆ k : ŌäĢ, k Ōēź 2 ŌåÆ
    Ōłā (x : Fin k ŌåÆ ŌäĢ) (d : ŌäĢ), d > 0 Ōł¦
    (ŌłĆ i, IsSquare (x i)) Ōł¦
    (ŌłĆ i j : Fin k, i < j ŌåÆ x i < x j) Ōł¦
    (ŌłĆ (i : ŌäĢ) (hi : i + 1 < k),
      x Ō¤©i, by omegaŌ¤® + d Ōēż x Ō¤©i + 1, hiŌ¤® Ōł¦
      x Ō¤©i + 1, hiŌ¤® Ōēż x Ō¤©i, by omegaŌ¤® + d + C) := by
  sorry

/--
Erd┼æs Problem 782, Part 2 (Combinatorial cubes in the squares) [BEF90]:

For any $m$, the set of perfect squares contains a combinatorial cube of
dimension $m$: there exist $a$ and positive $b_1, \ldots, b_m$ such that
$a + \sum_{i \in S} b_i$ is a perfect square for every subset
$S \subseteq \{1, \ldots, m\}$.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
theorem erdos_782.variants.cubes : answer(sorry) Ōåö
    ŌłĆ m : ŌäĢ, Ōłā (a : ŌäĢ) (b : Fin m ŌåÆ ŌäĢ),
    (ŌłĆ i, b i > 0) Ōł¦
    (ŌłĆ S : Finset (Fin m), IsSquare (a + Ōłæ i Ōłł S, b i)) := by
  sorry

end Erdos782

    46ŌåÆ
    47ŌåÆ### `answer()` usage
    48ŌåÆ
    49ŌåÆThe original problem (per the source and docstrings) asks two yes/no questions: "Does there exist a constant C such thatŌĆ”" and "Do the squares contain arbitrarily large cube configurations?" ŌĆö both are **Case 1** (yes/no).
    50ŌåÆ

file.content:
# Review: `deepmind/782.lean` ŌĆö Erd┼æs Problem 782

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erd┼æs Problem 782`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/782`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (quasi-progressions in squares, relationship between the two parts).
- [x] Bibliography entry present: `[BEF90]` with full citation (Brown, Erd┼æs, Freedman, 1990).

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos782 ... end Erdos782`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos782`.
- [x] `open Finset` and `open scoped BigOperators` appear **before** the namespace block.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- N/A ŌĆö no `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

### `erdos_782` (main theorem, line 46)

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_782` (snake_case matching namespace number).
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 5 11]`.
- [x] Has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`) referencing the source `[BEF90]`.

### `erdos_782.variants.cubes` (variant, line 65)

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Named `erdos_782.variants.cubes` following the `erdos_N.variants.<descriptor>` convention.
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 5 11]`.
- [x] Has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`) referencing `[BEF90]`.

### `answer()` usage

The original problem (per the source and docstrings) asks two yes/no questions: "Does there exist a constant C such thatŌĆ”" and "Do the squares contain arbitrarily large cube configurations?" ŌĆö both are **Case 1** (yes/no).

- [x] Both theorems use `answer(sorry) Ōåö P` format, correct for yes/no questions.
- [x] Problem is open, so `answer(sorry)` (not `answer(True)` / `answer(False)`) is correct.
- [x] The RHS of each `Ōåö` formalizes the question as originally asked, so `answer(True)` would mean "yes" and `answer(False)` would mean "no".
- [x] All quantifiers appear **after** `answer(sorry)` on the RHS of `Ōåö`, not as theorem parameters.

### Mathematical correctness

**Part 1 (`erdos_782`):** Formalizes the existence of a universal constant C such that for any k Ōēź 2, one can find k perfect squares forming a quasi-progression with gap d > 0 and tolerance C. The conditions ŌĆö strict monotonicity, all terms are squares, and the quasi-progression bounds x_i + d Ōēż x_{i+1} Ōēż x_i + d + C ŌĆö faithfully capture the problem statement. Using `C : ŌäĢ` (rather than requiring C > 0 explicitly) is logically equivalent since C = 0 degenerates to arithmetic progressions, which are known not to exist in the squares for length Ōēź 4.

**Part 2 (`erdos_782.variants.cubes`):** Formalizes the existence of arbitrarily large combinatorial cubes in the squares: for any m, there exist a and positive bŌéü, ŌĆ”, bŌéś such that a + ╬Ż_{i Ōłł S} bßĄó is a perfect square for every subset S ŌŖå {1, ŌĆ”, m}. This matches the standard definition of a combinatorial cube. The requirement that all bßĄó > 0 is correctly included.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[category ...]` attribute.
- [x] Both use `research open`, which is a valid category value.
- [x] Both `research open` theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[AMS ...]` attribute (not multiple separate tags).
- [x] At least one AMS subject number is listed (two: `5` and `11`).
- [x] Numbers are in ascending numeric order: `5 11`.
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] Both `5` (combinatorics) and `11` (number theory) are valid AMS MSC2020 codes.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Both open conjectures use the multi-line `by\n  sorry` format (acceptable per checklist).
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
- [x] No informal proof sketches included.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant named `erdos_782.variants.cubes` following the `erdos_N.variants.<descriptor>` convention.
- [x] Variant lives in the **same file** as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category ..., AMS ...]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring describing it as Part 2 (combinatorial cubes in the squares).

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation for proof bodies; 4-space continuation indent for theorem statements (standard Lean style).
- [x] Unicode math symbols used throughout: `ŌłĆ`, `Ōłā`, `Ōłł`, `Ōåö`, `ŌåÆ`, `ŌäĢ`, `Ōēź`, `Ōēż`, `Ōł¦`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` directives.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` ŌĆö **not verified** (requires full project build).
- [ ] Custom linters ŌĆö **not verified** (requires build). No violations are anticipated based on code inspection.

---

## Summary

**Result: PASS** (pending build verification)

The file is well-structured and conforms to all inspectable checklist items. Both the main theorem and the variant faithfully formalize the two parts of Erd┼æs Problem 782 (quasi-progressions and combinatorial cubes in perfect squares). The `answer()` usage correctly follows Case 1 for yes/no questions. Docstrings are clear and reference the source paper. No issues found.

content.text: The review result is **PASS**. No changes needed.
